paragraphs.text: After this Moses says: “And the whole earth was of one lip, and of one speech for all… And they said: Come, let us build us a city and a tower, whose top will reach unto heaven; and let us make us a name, lest we be scattered abroad upon the face of the whole earth” (Gen 11:1, 4). Why do they build a fortified city, when there was no one for them to fear? Why a tower reaching to the heavens, when an unalterable covenant had been given them that there would not again be a flood? They say, “lest we be scattered abroad upon the face of the whole earth.” Who would scatter them, when besides them there was no one? But from what they said, “let us make us a name,” it is revealed that pride and arrogance moved them to build the city and the tower, and their building was halted by the division which arose between them.

“And the Lord came down to see the city and the tower” — that is, God saw the deed of their folly. “And He said… they have begun to do this: and now nothing will be restrained from them” (Gen 11:5–6) — that is, the very thing they intended will not fail: that they will not be delivered from the punishment they feared. They said, “lest we be scattered” — and this very thing shall befall them. “Come, let us go down, and there confound their language” (Gen 11:7). These words are spoken not to a single person, for it would not have been fitting for a single person to say, “come… let us confound.” So speaks God the Father to the Son and to the Spirit, because in the first time, as in the last, the gift of tongues is imparted not without the Son and the Spirit.

“Let us confound their language, that they may not understand one another’s speech” (Gen 11:7). It is likely that each tribe began to speak a particular language which had been given it. If they had not lost their original language, the work they had begun would not have ceased. But with the loss of the original language by all the tribes except one tribe, with which it remained, the building they had begun ceased. The new languages so estranged the tribes from one another that they could not understand one another; and from the division produced by the difference of languages came their wars. Such was the cause of the wars among those who, fearing attacks, had built a fortified city — and so those who feared this very thing are scattered over all the earth. In the scattering of the tribes Nimrod too took part, who seized Babylon and was the first to reign in it — for if he had not striven to scatter the tribes one after another, he could not have seized the homeland common to all.

Then Moses begins again to number the generations from Noah to Abraham and says: Noah begot Shem and his brothers; Shem begot Arphaxad; Arphaxad begot Cainan; Cainan begot Salah; Salah begot Eber; Eber begot Peleg; Peleg begot Reu (Ragav); Reu begot Serug; Serug begot Nahor; Nahor begot Terah; Terah begot Abram, Nahor, and Haran; Haran begot Lot, Milcah, and Iscah, that is Sarai, who was so called because of her beauty. Both of these afterward became the wives of their uncles. Terah took his son Abram and his grandson Lot and his daughter-in-law Sarai out of Ur of the Chaldees, came to Haran, and dwelt there (Gen 11:31).
